metadata.from: Melio A. Tonini, Attorney and Counselor at Law
metadata.to: The Heirs of Frank J. Warnock, Deceased
people_mentioned: Ralph W. Hochhaiser (or similar — partially obscured)
metadata.mood_tone: Formal and businesslike, with some urgency regarding deadlines and decisions about the sale of property; mildly advisory in tone regarding the heirs' options.
metadata.legibility_notes: Page 2 has significant bleed-through from the reverse side making some text difficult to read, particularly the top portion. One name in page 1 (the purchaser of the 80-acre tract) is partially obscured by correction fluid/strikethrough. Page 4 has a large stain obscuring parts of the right column.
metadata.historical_context: Post-WWII Iowa legal/estate matter from 1954. The Hanifen family members (Robert Hanifen, John R. Hanifen, James Ed. Hanifen) appear in the Table of Heirship as heirs of Ollie Miller, connecting this letter to the Hanifen family correspondence collection.

pages.transcription:
MELIO A. TONINI
ATTORNEY AND COUNSELOR AT LAW
1000 FLEMING BUILDING
DES MOINES 9, IOWA
TELEPHONE 4-2215

December 21, 1954

TO: THE HEIRS OF FRANK J. WARNOCK, DECEASED.

This letter is written to you as a supplement to my letter of December 17, 1954. You are advised that since that letter, the 80 acre tract of land now owned by Thompson [and Evans has been purchased by one Ralph W. Hochhaiser? — partially obscured by strikethrough]. He has also made an offer on the 40 acre tract of land on which the house is situated. For this property, he originally made an offer of $125.00 per acre, but I have now succeeded in getting him up to $150.00 per acre. The offer has been submitted through the Larson Real Estate Agency of Madrid, Iowa, real estate brokers. Consequently a commission of 5% will have to be paid to the Larson Agency.

Also as stated in my previous letter, there would have to be deducted costs of special administration, fees of special administration and attorney fees allowed me as attorney for the contestents, plus abstracting expenses and any other expenses incident to the sale, including taxes.

Because the purchaser of the 80 acre tract of land has already consumated the purchase of that part, it is very possible I will be able to get him to raise his offer on our tract. Personally, I feel that this is a good time to make the sale, inasmuch as we cannot get insurance on the buildings or the house, and they could be destroyed by fire. Eventho the sale should be consumated we would have to wait until March 1st or a short time thereafter before we could collect all of the money because we would not be in a position where we could give good title until the estate has been closed fully.

I don't know what your personal view-points may be but from my knowledge of the property, I think we are getting to the point where we cannot expect much more.

I have heard rumors that some of the heirs might want to renounce their share in favor of other heirs. Of course I want you to know that unless I hear from you I will make out the checks at the conclusion of everything just as the law provides.

Please advise me of your intentions in this regard. Also advise me if I have your address incorrectly set out. I must hear from you on or before December 30, 1954.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
MELIO A. TONINI

MAT/jg

pages.page_label: page 2 — Letter dated December 17, 1954 (front, with bleed-through from reverse)
pages.transcription:
[Note: The top portion of this page shows heavy bleed-through text from the reverse (page 3), making that section largely illegible as a distinct layer. The primary text of the December 17, 1954 letter begins below the letterhead.]

MELIO A. TONINI
ATTORNEY AND COUNSELOR AT LAW
1000 FLEMING BUILDING
DES MOINES 9, IOWA
TELEPHONE 4-2215

December 17, 1954

TO THE HEIRS OF FRANK J. WARNOCK, DECEASED:

This is to advise you of the status of the Estate of Frank J. Warnock, deceased. As you all know, Mr. Warnock executed a will on May 20, 1953 in which he gave all of his property, both real and personal to Mr. Hershel Evans, now of near Winterset, Iowa, and Mr. Pete Thompson of Woodward, Iowa. Objections were filed to the probate of that will on behalf of those heirs who engaged me as attorney and also on behalf of all other heirs, inasmuch as the Court appointed me as attorney for all heirs, who had no representation.

The objections to the will of Frank J. Warnock were based on the ground that he was of unsound mind at the time he executed the will and also on the ground that undo influence had been used by the legatees named in the will. The case came on for trial on November 29, 1954, at the Boone County Court House. It continued through Monday, Tuesday and Wednesday. During the afternoon of Wednesday, an offer was made by the other side, offering to settle on the basis of the heirs taking 40 acres on which the house was situated, subject to the payment of the costs of special administration from that 40 acres. At that point we had to consider whether there was any opportunity for us to win the case. Based on the testimony which we had introduced there was absolutely no reason for believing that we could ever get to a jury. A Stipulation was therefore entered into on the basis of settlement for that 40 acres, and I was made trustee for the heirs. I accordingly have a Quit Claim Deed from both Thompson and Evans and their respective wives to that 40 acres. Of course you realize that the 40 acres which we received in settlement is not as good land as the other land across the road. After the sale of this 40 acres, I don't believe that any of us will be enriched very much, due to the fact that expenses of special administration are deducted therefrom and also attorney fees.

My last advice has been that Mr. Thompson and Mr. Evans were selling their 80 acres, at least I have understood that from a realtor in Madrid. If this is so it might be convenient to sell ours at the same time, in the hopes that the purchaser would want a total of 120 acres with improvements thereon. Although the improvements on our 40 acres are nothing to shout about, they can be made useable.

To those of you who have not been advised heretofore, I want you to know of the members of your family who have been especially helpful in this matter. It was through Agnes Warnock Graham that I first leaned of the death of this man, and consequently ascertained that there was a will leaving out all of the heirs. Pauline Miller was then appointed special administratrix. Since we had a relative as special administratrix, it gave us access to certain evidence

pages.page_label: page 3 — Letter dated December 17, 1954 (continuation, page 2 of letter)
pages.transcription:
-2-

that was discovered in the house that we would not otherwise have had. Mr. & Mrs. Robert Warnock, Mrs. Harriett Martin and Pauline Miller and Phil Miller were in constant attendance at the trial in Boone. Many of you took no part in this proceeding actively and yet you will participate in the results on an equal basis with those who assisted materially.

In order that you will understand the proportion to which you will be an heir, I am attaching a seperate sheet. If you have any questions regarding this record of inheritance, please advise me. On the other hand, if I have made an error in any way, I must also be advised. You understand of course that inasmuch as Elizabeth Brookhart died leaving no children and her husband is deceased, that none of her heirs receive anything. Harriett Martin as a surviving sister receives 1/4th and then the heirs of Albert Warnock, Evelyn Reed and Ollie Miller each participate in the 1/4th that would have gone to their respective father or mother. By this I mean that the heirs of Albert Warnock will divide a fourth, the heirs of Evelyn Reed will divide a fourth, and the heirs of Ollie Miller will divide a fourth. Horace Donald Warnock, being dead, his share will be divided between his children. Likewise the children of Earl Miller and the children of Peal Miller will divide the share that would have gone to their deceased parent.

If any of you have any ideas on how this property should be sold, please advise me immediately. I can tell you that it can be sold at private sale or at public sale. I am against, at this time, selling the same at public sale because I am certain that we would receive less. I am also personally convinced that if Thompson and Evans sell their 80 acres, we could probably get more out of our 40, selling it at the same time, but I would have to investigate this very thoroughly. I might tell you that the inheritance tax appraisers for the State of Iowa, have appraised our 40 at $75.00 per acre. This of course seems a ridiculously low figure and I feel that we should get around $150.00 per acre. Of course you understand that the appraisal of the inheritance tax appraisers is only for inheritance tax purposes.

Again I want to say that if you have any questions, please direct them to the undersigned as soon as possible. I am hoping that each of you get a copy of this as I am sending them to the last known address that I have.

Heirs of Evelyn Reed.......... Very truly yours, ..........1/4th
Glen Reed
Alvin Reed
Harriett Kiehm

[signature]
MELIO A. TONINI

MAT/jg

pages.page_label: page 4 — Table of Heirship (attachment to December 17, 1954 letter)
pages.transcription:
TABLE OF HEIRSHIP

After payment of all expenses the balance is divided as follows:

HARRIETT MARTIN.........................................1/4th

Heirs of ALBERT WARNOCK................................1/4th

        John Gilbert Warnock    1/6th of 1/4
        Robert Dale Warnock     1/6th of 1/4
        Frank Harold Warnock    1/6th of 1/4
        Florence Margaret "     1/6th of 1/4
        Ruth Alice Richman      1/6th of 1/4
        James Warnock           1/18th of 1/4
        Charles Albert Warnock  1/18th of 1/4
        John Warnock            1/18th of 1/4

Heirs of OLLIE MILLER..................................1/4th

        Cecil Miller            1/7th of 1/4
        Francis Miller          1/7th of 1/4
        Lloyd Miller            1/7th of 1/4
        Alvin Miller            1/7th of 1/4
        Pauline Miller          1/7th of 1/4
        Warren Miller           1/14th of 1/4
        Marilyn Mitchell        1/14th of 1/4
        Robert Hanifen          1/21st of 1/4
        John R. Hanifen         1/21st of 1/4
        James Ed. Hanifen       1/21st of 1/4

Heirs of Evelyn Reed...................................1/4th

        Glen Reed               1/3rd of 1/4
        Alvin Reed              1/3rd of 1/4
        Harriett Kiehm          1/3rd of 1/4

MELIO A. TONINI
ATTORNEY AND COUNSELOR AT LAW
1000 FLEMING BUILDING
DES MOINES 9, IOWA
TELEPHONE 4-2215

December 17, 1954

summary: This document consists of two letters (dated December 17 and December 21, 1954) and an attached Table of Heirship from Des Moines attorney Melio A. Tonini to the heirs of Frank J. Warnock, deceased. The December 17 letter explains the outcome of a will contest trial at Boone County Court House, in which the heirs settled for 40 acres of land (rather than the full estate) after Warnock's will left everything to Hershel Evans and Pete Thompson; Tonini was made trustee for the heirs. The December 21 supplemental letter reports that a buyer (possibly Ralph Hochhaiser) purchased the adjacent 80-acre tract and has offered $150/acre for the heirs' 40 acres through the Larson Real Estate Agency in Madrid, Iowa, with a response deadline of December 30, 1954. Significantly for the Hanifen family collection, the Table of Heirship identifies Robert Hanifen, John R. Hanifen, and James Ed. Hanifen as heirs of Ollie Miller, each receiving 1/21st of 1/4th of the estate.
